schools.summary: Paul opens with a threefold self-designation — a servant (doulos, bondservant, wholly owned by Christ, Robertson), called to be an apostle (by divine call, not man, Galatians 1:1), separated (aphorizo, set apart) unto the gospel of God; both humility and divine commission.
Chrysostom and Theodoret: Paul was set apart for the gospel as at his conversion and commission (Acts 9:15; 13:2); the gospel 'of God,' its author God himself (with Calvin).

themes: Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ
called to be an apostle, separated unto the gospel of God
the threefold designation
humility and divine commission

schools.summary: The gospel was promised afore by God through his prophets in the holy Scriptures — no novelty but the fulfillment of the Old Testament promise (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Theodoret); the continuity and unity of the Testaments.

themes: which he had promised afore by his prophets
in the holy scriptures
the gospel's ancient promise
the continuity of the Testaments

schools.summary: The gospel concerns God's Son, Jesus Christ our Lord, made of the seed of David according to the flesh — his human nature descended from David (fulfilling the Messianic promise), 'according to the flesh' implying another nature besides the human (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the two natures — divinity and humanity both in Christ for salvation, the divinity possessing power and righteousness conveyed to us through the humanity; the whole gospel included in Christ.

themes: concerning his Son Jesus Christ our Lord
of the seed of David according to the flesh
the gospel's subject the Son
Christ's true humanity and Davidic descent

schools.summary: Christ was declared (horizo, marked out, defined, proved) to be the Son of God with power by the resurrection — according to the spirit of holiness (his higher nature, in antithesis to 'according to the flesh,' Ellicott, Robertson); the resurrection definitely marked Jesus off as God's Son, vindicating his claims (Robertson).

Gill, Calvin: not made but manifested — Christ, the eternal Son, was declared, proved, and marked out as the Son of God by the celestial power of the Spirit in his resurrection (he rose by his own power, John 2:19; 10:18).

debates.question: Does 'declared the Son of God' mean he became or was manifested as the Son?

sides.position: Manifested, proved, marked out (horizo) — not made the Son, but powerfully declared to be what he eternally was, by the resurrection

themes: declared to be the Son of God with power
according to the spirit of holiness, by the resurrection
not made but manifested
the resurrection sealing his Sonship

schools.summary: Paul received grace and apostleship (grace his favour as a Christian, apostleship his peculiar office, Ellicott) for the obedience of faith among all nations, for Christ's name — 'obedience of faith' meaning the obedience which springs from faith, faith itself the obedient surrender (Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin: gratuitous apostleship, wholly of divine favour not his own worth; the chief act of obedience to God is faith, and the chief disobedience unbelief — faith is properly that by which we obey the gospel.

debates.question: Does 'obedience of faith' mean obedience to a creed or the obedience that is faith?

sides.position: The obedience which springs from faith — faith itself the act of obedient surrender, not assent to a body of doctrine

themes: grace and apostleship
for obedience to the faith among all nations, for his name
Paul's commission
faith as obedient surrender

schools.summary: The Romans were among the nations to whom Paul was sent, now 'the called of Jesus Christ' — called by and belonging to Christ (Gill), once in Gentile darkness now called (Theodoret); within Paul's commission to the nations.
Calvin: the called are not merely those to whom the gospel is addressed, but those effectually called, made partakers of Christ — chosen by the Father, committed to Christ's care.

themes: among whom are ye also
the called of Jesus Christ
the Romans' inclusion among the called
the effectual call

schools.summary: Paul addresses all in Rome, beloved of God and called to be saints (holy ones, set apart), with his characteristic greeting — grace (God's free favour, the source) and peace (the fruit, reconciliation) — from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ jointly, a witness to Christ's deity placed beside the Father (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the calling to be saints flows from God's love — the Romans beloved of God, and thence called and consecrated.

themes: beloved of God, called to be saints
grace to you and peace from God our Father, and the Lord Jesus Christ
the apostolic greeting
Christ joined with the Father as source of blessing

schools.summary: Paul begins with thanksgiving to God through Jesus Christ (the Mediator) for the Romans, whose faith was spoken of throughout the whole world — famed everywhere, in the capital of the empire, a testimony to the gospel's power.
Chrysostom (with Henry): Paul's wisdom and love — beginning with praise of their faith, winning their hearts, and giving God the glory; thanksgiving ascending through Christ the Mediator.

themes: I thank my God through Jesus Christ for you all
your faith is spoken of throughout the whole world
Paul's thanksgiving
the renown of the Roman church's faith

schools.summary: Paul solemnly calls God to witness that, serving him with his spirit (the whole inward man, not mere ceremony, Robertson, Calvin) in the gospel of his Son, he made unceasing mention of the Romans in his prayers — his love expressed in faithful, constant intercession.

themes: God is my witness, whom I serve with my spirit
without ceasing I make mention of you in my prayers
the spiritual service of God
Paul's constant intercession

schools.summary: Paul prayed that, by some means and at last, he might have a prosperous journey, by the will of God, to come to the Romans — a long-cherished desire submitted to God's good pleasure; the providence that would bring him, though as a prisoner (Acts 28).
Calvin: Paul subordinated his desire to God's will — longing to come, yet submitting the matter to God's good pleasure; he would come if and as God willed.

themes: if by any means I might have a prosperous journey
by the will of God to come unto you
Paul's submissive prayer
desire yielded to God's will

schools.summary: Paul longed to see the Romans (a deep, tender affection) that he might impart some spiritual gift (charisma, some grace or benefit, not material but spiritual, Barnes, Calvin) to the end they might be established — confirmed and strengthened in the faith.

themes: I long to see you
that I may impart unto you some spiritual gift
to the end ye may be established
Paul's pastoral aim

schools.summary: Paul gracefully corrects himself (lest he seem to assume superiority, Calvin, Barnes, Robertson) — the comfort would be mutual, he and the Romans encouraged together by their shared faith, the apostle himself drawing comfort from their faith as they from his; the humility and mutual edification of the body.

themes: that I may be comforted together with you
by the mutual faith both of you and me
Paul's modest correction
mutual edification and humility

schools.summary: Paul had often purposed to come to the Romans but was hindered ('let,' in the old sense, prevented until now by various providences, his work elsewhere, Robertson) — desiring to have some fruit (converts, edification, the harvest of his ministry) among them as among the other Gentiles.

themes: oftentimes I purposed to come unto you, but was let hitherto
that I might have some fruit among you
Paul's repeated, hindered purpose
the desire for fruit at Rome

schools.summary: Paul regarded himself a debtor (under obligation, having received the gospel and his commission, Barnes, Robertson) to all — Greeks and Barbarians, wise and unwise — the two pairs encompassing all mankind, none excluded; the gospel a debt owed to all regardless of culture or learning.

themes: I am debtor both to the Greeks, and to the Barbarians
both to the wise, and to the unwise
Paul's universal obligation
the gospel owed to all

schools.summary: Paul, as far as in him lay, was ready and eager to preach the gospel even at Rome — the proud capital of the empire, undeterred by its splendor or scorn; leading into his declaration that he is not ashamed of the gospel (v.16).
Calvin: the boldness — Paul ready to bring the despised gospel to the seat of worldly power, not ashamed of it.

themes: I am ready to preach the gospel to you that are at Rome
Paul's readiness to preach at Rome
eagerness and boldness
bringing the gospel to the capital

schools.summary: Paul is not ashamed of the gospel (though a stumbling-block to Jews and folly to Greeks, he glories in it), for it is the power of God (dynamis, the mighty saving agency, not a dead letter, Robertson) unto salvation to every one that believes — faith the condition — to the Jew first (in privilege and order, Acts 13:46) and also to the Greek (all the Gentiles).

Calvin: the gospel is God's power unto salvation because by it God reveals his righteousness; offered to all, but its saving power appears only where the Spirit illuminates the heart — to others it is the savour of death by their own wickedness.

themes: I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ
the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth
to the Jew first, and also to the Greek
the theme verse of the epistle

schools.summary: The 'righteousness of God' is God's plan or method of justifying men by faith (Barnes, Ellicott, Calvin) — God's way of accounting men righteous, not his attribute of justice nor mere goodness; revealed 'from faith to faith' (faith first to last, Robertson), proved by Habakkuk 2:4, 'the just shall live by faith.'

Gill, Henry: the imputed righteousness of Christ — the righteousness Christ wrought by obedience and death, imputed to believers, called 'of God' by its author and approval; faith all in all, both in the beginning and progress of the Christian life.

Wesley: the phrase can mean God's essential righteousness (justice and mercy), but here especially the righteousness of faith — so called because God found out, reveals, gives, approves, and crowns it; 'from faith to faith' by a series of clearer promises.

sides.position: God's plan/method of justifying men by faith — his way of accounting men righteous
The imputed righteousness of Christ, reckoned to believers

themes: the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith
the just shall live by faith
justification by faith
the central crux of the epistle

schools.summary: The wrath of God (orge — not passion or revenge but his settled, holy displeasure against sin, 'the wrath of reason and law,' Barnes, Clarke, Robertson) is revealed from heaven against ungodliness (against God) and unrighteousness (against men, the two tables) of those who hold down (suppress, hinder) the truth in unrighteousness — imprisoning the truth they have lest it restrain them.

Calvin: by ungodliness and unrighteousness one thing is chiefly designated — ingratitude toward God, impiety dishonoring him and unrighteousness robbing him of his glory; the wrath of God filling the whole world, leaving none unpunished.

themes: the wrath of God is revealed from heaven
against all ungodliness and unrighteousness
who hold the truth in unrighteousness
God's holy displeasure against sin

schools.summary: That which may be known of God (his existence and many perfections, the natural knowledge available to all, not the full saving knowledge, Barnes, Robertson) is manifest in them, for God himself has shown it — implanting an awareness of his being and displaying his glory in creation (Henry).

Calvin: God has presented to all the means of knowing him, so manifesting himself by his works that men must see there is a God; but this knowledge avails only to remove excuse, not to save — it differs greatly from the knowledge that brings salvation.

themes: that which may be known of God is manifest in them
for God hath shewed it unto them
natural revelation
knowledge enough to condemn, not to save

schools.summary: God's invisible attributes — his eternal power and Godhead (theiotes, divine nature/properties, not theotes, deity/personality, Robertson, Ellicott) — are clearly seen, discerned by the mind through the visible creation (the unseen seen through the seen), so that men are without excuse.

Calvin: the manifestation is clear enough that men cannot plead ignorance; though by our blindness it suffices not to save, we are not so blind as to have any excuse for our perverseness — natural revelation removes excuse but does not bring salvation.

themes: the invisible things of him are clearly seen by the things that are made
his eternal power and Godhead
so that they are without excuse
the proof from creation

schools.summary: The heathen, when they knew God (had real knowledge, the philosophers especially, Barnes, Robertson), glorified him not as God nor gave thanks — ingratitude and refusal to honor him the root of corruption — but became vain in their reasonings (the empty speculations of the philosophers) and their foolish heart (the whole inner man) was darkened.

Calvin: having forsaken the truth of God, they turned to the vanity of their own reason; their unrighteousness was this — they quickly choked by their own depravity the seed of right knowledge before it ripened.

themes: when they knew God, they glorified him not
became vain in their imaginations, and their foolish heart was darkened
the declension from knowledge into folly
ingratitude the root of corruption

schools.summary: The heathen, professing to be wise (the philosophers boasting of wisdom, Barnes, Robertson), became fools — the folly judicially inflicted, they were 'made fools,' their pretended wisdom turning to folly (an oxymoron, Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin: not only the philosophers but all men sought to be wise of themselves, drawing God down to their own conception — the arrogance is innate; the wiser they thought themselves, the more foolish they became.

themes: professing themselves to be wise, they became fools
the heathen's self-conceited folly
wisdom apart from God is folly
the judicial folly inflicted on pride

schools.summary: The heathen exchanged the glory of the incorruptible God for images (not changing God himself but forsaking him, the truth of God for a lie) — a descending scale from corruptible man (Greek/Roman gods) down to birds, four-footed beasts, and creeping things (the Egyptian ibis, ox, crocodile, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott).

Theodoret: the height of folly — they worshipped as gods the very creatures men eat, loathe as unclean, or kill as noxious; with Calvin, idolatry befouls God's majesty by forming him in the likeness of base things.

themes: changed the glory of the uncorruptible God into an image
like to corruptible man, and to birds, and fourfooted beasts, and creeping things
the descent into idolatry
the truth of God exchanged for a lie

schools.summary: God 'gave them up' (paredoken) — abandoned them, withdrew his restraint, and suffered them to follow their own lusts to uncleanness, not actively inducing sin but leaving them to themselves (Barnes, Robertson; the withdrawal of restraint sent men deeper down).

Theodoret: 'gave them up' means 'permitted' — seeing them unwilling to be led to the Creator, God deprived them of his special providence and suffered them to be carried about like an unsteady vessel.

Calvin: more than bare permission — God by his just judgment so arranges that men are led into sin by their own lusts and by Satan his executioner; yet God is not the author of sin, whose roots abide in the sinner himself.

debates.question: Does 'God gave them up' mean mere permission or active judgment?

sides.position: Permission — God withdrew his restraint and left men to their own lusts
More than permission — an efficient judicial act, yet God not the author of sin

themes: God also gave them up to uncleanness
through the lusts of their own hearts
the judicial abandonment
sin punished by sin

schools.summary: The heathen changed the truth of God into a lie (the true God exchanged for false gods, a Hebraism, Barnes, Robertson, Calvin) and worshipped and served the creature rather than (para, to the neglect of) the Creator — the fundamental inversion of idolatry; Paul turns aside in reverent doxology, 'who is blessed for ever. Amen' (Ellicott, Robertson).

Theodoret: impiety the foundation of their iniquities; and the same reproach lies against those who call the only-begotten Son a creature yet worship him — they ought either to rank him with God who begat him, or, calling him a creature, not adore him.

themes: changed the truth of God into a lie
worshipped and served the creature more than the Creator
the inversion of idolatry
Paul's reverent doxology

schools.summary: For the cause of their idolatry, God gave them up a second time — to vile affections, passions of dishonor contrary to nature (Robertson, Calvin), the practical fruit of their false belief, sins abundantly attested by the heathen writers themselves; Paul's faithful diagnosis of the heathen world.

Chrysostom: these affections were vile, the soul more dishonored than the body in disease; Paul deprives them of excuse by noting they 'changed' the natural use, having possessed it — the sin of a perverted, obstinate determination, not mere ignorance.

themes: God gave them up unto vile affections
their women did change the natural use into that which is against nature
the second giving up
idolatry issuing in degradation

schools.summary: Paul, for his argument, faithfully charges the heathen world with these shameful, unnatural sins — degrading, yet abundantly documented in the Greek and Roman writers themselves (Barnes, Robertson) — and notes they received the recompense of their error: sin its own punishment, the degradation itself a judgment (Ellicott, Clarke).
Theodoret: infamy is the severest penalty of such affections; what no victorious enemy could inflict, they willingly ran into, bringing on themselves a punishment heavier than any judge would impose.

themes: men with men working that which is unseemly
receiving in themselves that recompence of their error which was meet
the continued degradation
sin its own recompense

schools.summary: The wordplay: as they did not approve (edokimasan) to retain God, God gave them over to a reprobate (adokimon) mind — a mind disapproved, void of judgment (Robertson, Ellicott, Calvin); a willful rejection, not inability but displeasure with God (Barnes, Clarke), issuing in deeds not fitting.

Chrysostom: the sin belonged to the mind, not the flesh (against the heretics) — the fount of the evils flowed from a corrupted, undiscerning mind; their daring deeds came of practice and perverted determination, not ignorance.

themes: they did not like to retain God in their knowledge
God gave them over to a reprobate mind
the third giving up
the wordplay of rejection and judgment

schools.summary: The reprobate mind issued in being 'filled with all unrighteousness' — the intensive force, the heathen world saturated with sin (Chrysostom, Robertson) — followed by a catalogue: wickedness, covetousness, maliciousness, envy, murder, debate (strife), deceit, malignity, whisperers (the best manuscripts omitting 'fornication,' Ellicott).

Calvin: Paul set down these vices as they occurred, not in strict order; none was free from all, though not all guilty of each — every one chargeable with some, so all are constrained to acknowledge their faults.

themes: being filled with all unrighteousness
envy, murder, debate, deceit, malignity, whisperers
the catalogue of sins
the moral ruin from the reprobate mind

schools.summary: The catalogue continues — backbiters (open slanderers, against the secret whisperers), haters of God (active, enmity against their Maker), despiteful (insolent, injurious), proud, boasters, inventors of evil things, disobedient to parents (violating the most natural bond) — a comprehensive indictment of the heathen character (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).

themes: backbiters, haters of God, despiteful, proud, boasters
inventors of evil things, disobedient to parents
the continued catalogue
comprehensive corruption

schools.summary: The catalogue closes — without understanding (senseless, void of moral discernment), covenantbreakers (faithless), without natural affection (lacking even the love of kindred, the extreme of corruption), implacable (perhaps omitted in the best manuscripts, Ellicott), unmerciful (without pity); the heathen sunk so low as to lose even nature's bonds (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).

themes: without understanding, covenantbreakers
without natural affection, implacable, unmerciful
the close of the catalogue
the breaking of nature's bonds

schools.summary: The heathen knew the judgment of God — that such deeds deserved death (by conscience and natural law, their sin against knowledge, inexcusable, Barnes, Robertson) — yet not only did them but had pleasure in those who did them: the climax of wickedness.

Calvin (with Barnes, Robertson): to approve and delight in others' sin is worse than to sin oneself — passion might excuse the act, but pleasure in evil shows a mind wholly given to it, a settled deliberate approval, the deepest depravity.

themes: knowing the judgment of God, that they which commit such things are worthy of death
not only do the same, but have pleasure in them that do them
the climactic charge
knowing approval of sin the deepest depravity
